literal: doing, performing, the verbal action itself; from √kṛ
invariant_definition: *Kriyā* names the verbal action itself — the dynamic process expressed by a finite verb — as distinct from its agent (*kartṛ*), instrument (*karaṇa*), or patient (*karman*/*kārya*). In Pāṇinian grammar a sentence is built around a *kriyā* whose role-relations are filled by the *kāraka*s; *kriyā* is therefore the relational center that the *kāraka*-roles surround. In ritual and Vedāntic discourse *kriyā* often picks out enjoined ritual performance under *codanā* (Pūrva-Mīmāṃsā) — but the term is broader, covering any process intelligible through agent-instrument-patient analysis. The *kriyā / kāraka / phala* triad shows how the Indian schools refused to treat 'action' as a primitive: every action is already a relational structure with its grammar and its causal residue. In Tantric and Śaiva contexts *kriyā* further names the *kriyā-pāda* of the Āgamas — the ritual-procedural section, contrasted with *jñāna-pāda* (doctrinal), *yoga-pāda* (meditative), and *caryā-pāda* (conduct). Vedāntic schools differ on whether *kriyā* in its ritual sense is co-instrumental in liberation (the *jñāna-karma-samuccaya* dispute), but they all accept the Pāṇinian grammatical analysis as the descriptive baseline for what an action is.

per_school.definition: *Kriyā* is the verbal action expressed by the finite verb (*tiṅanta*); the *kāraka* roles are its participants. Bhartṛhari's *Vākyapadīya* analyzes *kriyā* as a sequence (*krama*) of phases unified under a single *sādhya*; the apparently temporally-spread action is grasped as a single relational structure by the hearer's *pratibhā*.

*Kriyā* is paradigmatically the Veda-enjoined ritual act, intelligible through *bhāvanā* (the productive force connecting *codanā* — the injunction — to *phala*, the result). Mīmāṃsā distinguishes *śabda-bhāvanā* (the urge-producing force of the verb-ending) from *artha-bhāvanā* (the act's own causal contribution). *Apūrva* is posited to bridge the temporally completed *kriyā* and its later *phala*.
primary_loci: Kumārila, Tantra-Vārttika on bhāvanā [NOT YET RETRIEVED]

per_school.definition: All *kriyā* presupposes the agent-object division (*kartṛtva-bhoktṛtva*) constituted under *avidyā*; therefore no *kriyā*, however elevated, can directly produce the unproduced — namely the *ātman*'s self-knowledge. *Kriyā* nevertheless serves as *citta-śuddhi-upāya*. Bhartṛhari's analysis is accepted descriptively: the verb-centered relational structure is the right account of what action is at the *vyāvahārika* level.

*Kriyā* — including ritual *kriyā* — is real and continues for the realized as *bhagavad-ārādhana*. Rejecting Advaita's *jñāna-karma-samuccaya-nirāsa*, Rāmānuja treats *kriyā* offered to the Lord as itself an aspect of *bhakti-yoga*.

*Kriyā* is one of the three foundational *śakti*s of Śiva — *icchā* (will), *jñāna* (cognition), *kriyā* (action) — and is the energy through which differentiated manifestation actually unfolds. The Āgamic *kriyā-pāda* is correspondingly the ritual-procedural division. Crucially, *kriyā* is not a fall from *jñāna* but its enacted side; the realized *yogin*'s *spanda* itself is *kriyā-śakti* in undifferentiated freedom.

translator_note: 'Action' for *kriyā* is acceptable but loses the verb-centered relational structure that Pāṇinian and Mīmāṃsā analysis presuppose. 'Process' is sometimes better. The standard mistranslation collapses *kriyā* and *karma* into a single English 'action,' whereas in technical Sanskrit they are distinct: *kriyā* is the verbal process; *karman* is the patient role within it (or, derivatively, the residue the action produces). Translators who flatten the distinction destroy the entire *kāraka*-grammar argument.
